body_txt: Governments have the power to outright ban a work from being sold/displayed in their country. It usually happens in more authoritarian countries whose governments try to micromanage what their citizens can see. But even in more liberal countries, public pressure on retailers or broadcasters can lead to an effective ban on the work (although sometimes — but not always — you can get around it by importing it from somewhere else). Works that get this treatment tend to be excessively violent or sexual in nature, feature content that is considered "taboo" locally , criticize or mock the country or its government, inflame national or ethnic tensions, or otherwise depict behavior that might undermine the government's authority. The more authoritarian and/or conservative the government, the more works get censored. Even comparatively liberal countries have standards about what can be shown in media, which tends to affect the importation of erotica the most. This kind of thing is often associated with the government of the People's Republic of China, hence the trope name "Banned in China". But the name is actually a modern twist on the older term, "Banned in Boston " — the city was once a bastion of Moral Guardians , particularly with Puritan and Catholic influences, and a local "benevolent group" known as the Watch and Ward Society held immense sway over what could be displayed or sold in the city. Paradoxically, a ban in Boston could make a work more marketable elsewhere . Boston lost its puritanical reputation in the 1970s and is nowadays considered a fairly liberal area, politically if not culturally. China has taken up the mantle, given its frequent (and often arbitrary) censorship of anything it finds contrary to government policy or "harmful to the Chinese youth". As Hollywood focuses on overseas markets, they often have to water down their movies for China and other culturally conservative countries. Banned Episode is when this only happens to one or a few segments of a work, and may not be from a government. Compare New Media Are Evil , No Swastikas , Media Watchdog , Moral Guardians , and Values Dissonance . Bowdlerization can happen if the government requires substantial content editing before allowing the work to be imported. See Persona Non Grata for when people are banned from certain places. That hasn't stopped them from trying, though: Western Animation  Other   Music Newspapers Western Animation Other  China and North Korea are known for their extensive lists of bans, but Cambodia under the Khmer Rouge took things to an extreme by forbidding almost everything. Not only did they outlaw entertainment, art, and Khmer traditions, but they also abolished the economy, banned religion, and punished intellectualism with death. The most extreme ban was on something as simple as picking fruit , which was seen as an act of private ownership and contrary to their vision of a "classless" society . When Vietnam took control of Cambodia, all of the Khmer Rouge's bans were swiftly dismantled, particularly after Pol Pot's ill-fated decision to invade Vietnam's southern territories, which he fancied would make him a king in the style of the ancient Angkorian rulers . The Khmer Rouge's execution of countless intellectuals left a massive void, making recovery difficult. The cultural scene was left in ruins, and the economy limps along, sustained only by tourism. Films — Live-Action    Since 1959 (the time when Fidel Castro rose to power led by the Soviet Union), Cuba is the strictest country in the Western hemisphere in terms of censorship. It is also the only country in the Western hemisphere with a communist government. Films — Live-Action Video Games   Films — Live-Action   Films — Live-Action Toys  Films — Live-Action Western Animation Other  Anime & Manga Automobiles Comic Book Fashion Films — Live-Action Literature Live-Action TV Music Sports Stand-Up Comedy   Much of what's listed here was the work of a fascist military dictatorship in power in Greece between 1967 and 1974; it banned everything it deemed left-wing or a sign of modern decadence. This included labor unions, the peace symbol, rock music, "new math", miniskirts, and long hair on men. The craziest thing they banned was the letter "Z", which was used as a symbol of murdered resistance leader Grigoris Lambrakis ( zi meaning "he lives"). Films —Live-Action Literature Music Video Games  Films — Live-Action Literature Western Animation   Films — Live-Action Live-Action TV  Music  Technology  Video Games  Web Original  Western Animation   Films — Live-Action Professional Wrestling Technology Web Original  Any form of media seeking distribution in Iran needs the permission of the Ministry of Islamic Culture, which sets an arbitrary array of rules subject to change at any time by the government. These rules include any form of pornography or sexual imagery (particularly centered on the display of the female form, which is taboo in Islam), political material disagreeing with the government's goals, and any form of communication criticizing Islam. These restrictions are often circumvented by physical and internet piracy, use of satellite dishes, and illegal used book markets. General Films — Live-Action Literature Video Games   Many of the works listed here were banned up from after the 1916 Easter Rising due to the heavy influence of the Catholic Church in Ireland. Things such as teachings that didn't abide by Catholic Church ruling, feminism, sexual content, divorce, homosexuality, wedlock, and the like were among prohibited. However, eventually the Catholic Church's influence in the government started to lower down by the 1960s, and so did the Moral Guardians . Literature Music Video Games    Films — Live-Action Sports Toys  Films — Live-Action Web Original  Anime & Manga Films Literature Music Video Games Web Original Western Animation  Literature Western Animation  Music Other  Western Animation  General Advertising  Anime & Manga  Films — Live-Action  Literature  Web Animation  Web Original  Western Animation    Film  Comic Books Films — Live-Action Live-Action TV Professional Wrestling Toys Video Games Western Animation  General Anime & Manga Films — Live-Action Literature Music Theatre Video Games Web Original Western Animation Others  Films — Live-Action Literature Live-Action TV Music  While modern Russian, Belarusian, and Ukrainian censorship systems are relatively lax, back when these countries were united in the Soviet Union, it had a very extensive Culture Police -type system that inspired the current Chinese one. Foreign films were graded particularly harshly; domestic films with the following might get past, depending on the situation. It was not uncommon for domestic films to be created, widely screened, and then censored or banned as well (like the 1930 epic Earth , which was screened extensively and controversially before being censored). Films — Live-Action Literature Music  Comic Strips Literature Films — Live-Action Music  Films — Live-Action Music Tabletop Games Video Games   Thailand actively bans written works, social media posts and websites that criticize the Thai royal family, which is subject to one of the strictest lèse-majesté laws in the world . Under Thai law, authors who attack the Thai king are also subject to imprisonment. This is evident in the jailing of an Australian novelist in 2008. Films — Live-Action Magazines and Newspapers Music Theatre Video Games Web Original Western Animation  Anime & Manga Films — Live-Action Web Original Western Animation  Music Video Games   After the 2022 Russian invasion, Ukraine passed a law that prohibits Russian citizens from printing books in the country unless they renounce their Russian citizenship and take Ukrainian citizenship. The law also prohibits the import of books printed in Russia, Belarus, and the occupied Ukrainian territories, and requires a special authorisation for the import of Russian books from other countries. There's another law restricting the public performance of Russian music on Ukrainian television and radio, and they're only allowed to play if the musician is included in a "white list" of artists who have publicly condemned the Russian invasion of Ukraine. One reason is that Vietnam lacks the massive domestic tech ecosystem China has built. note Local company VNG's attempt at a homegrown social network initially succeeded but later faded into obscurity, becoming just a login hub for casual online games rather than a true Facebook rival. And even Vietnamese government officials actively use Facebook.
